_meta.description: Curated horror archetype library for the Archivist story generator. Each archetype describes a narrative *shape* (beats + setting hints + voice patterns) — NOT a public-domain text to copy. Seed archetypes are abstracted from the horror-genre conventions established by pre-1930 US horror (Poe, Lovecraft, Machen, Blackwood, Hodgson) and 1960s-70s paranormal case archives. The LLM uses these to generate ORIGINAL night-shift / rural-America / liminal-space stories in the Archivist persona.
_meta.source_posture: Archetypes describe genre conventions, not copied text. Seeded archetypes use only public-domain structural patterns; modern-sounding paraphrases were authored fresh for this repo.
anti_slop: Every archetype forbids gothic vocabulary ('verily', 'lantern', 'moor', 'doth', 'thee', 'ere'). Vesper's register is 2020s workplace/road prose, not 1890s cosmic horror.
Every archetype encodes mundane-anchor-then-wrong structure — the setup must feel plausibly real before the turn.
intentional_omissions: No archetype mentions named real people, dated real-world crimes, explicit self-harm methods, or minors as victims/perpetrators — the mod filter would reject these at output time anyway, and seeding them invites drift.

archetypes.setting_hint: long-haul trucker on an empty highway between 2 AM and 4 AM; rain, fog, or CB static common
key_beats: mundane-anchor: the narrator establishes routine (coffee, fuel stop, mileage)
first wrongness: something off-pattern (hitchhiker where none should be, headlights behind for too long, radio voice that names them)
escalation: the wrong thing persists or repeats
reveal or retreat: narrator either glimpses something that should not be there or abandons the route
voice_patterns: present-tense internal monologue, short sentences
avoid adjectives stacking; let the wrongness carry the weight
narrator doubts themselves before the reader does

archetypes.setting_hint: ER / ICU / psych ward during the slow 2-6 AM window; single-nurse floor or understaffed wing
key_beats: mundane-anchor: routine chart check or med round
patient or empty-room anomaly (room that should be empty shows light, patient names the wrong staff, equipment turns on)
narrator tests the anomaly rationally and fails
ambiguous end — narrator does NOT see the thing; they feel seen
voice_patterns: medical-terminology grounding
no screaming, no running — nurse voice stays measured
end on a mundane action interrupted by a small sensory detail

archetypes.setting_hint: two-lane county road at night, 50+ miles from the nearest town, reception dead
key_beats: mundane-anchor: rerouted by GPS or detour
landmark that shouldn't be there (unlisted town, deer-crossing sign in wrong language, abandoned gas station open)
person at the landmark who knows something about the narrator
narrator drives away; in the rearview, something is not right
voice_patterns: first-person, past tense, conversational
geographic specificity (county roads, mileposts) without naming the real state
end on the rearview mirror detail

archetypes.setting_hint: night-shift cashier / motel clerk / gas station attendant in a town under 3,000 people
key_beats: mundane-anchor: regular customer schedule
new customer whose behavior violates one small pattern
discovery that the customer's pattern matches something local (old photograph, missing-poster, closed-down place)
morning shift arrives; the narrator tries to explain and can't
voice_patterns: working-class idiom, sparse
prop details (register, camera, back room)
no explanation offered to the reader

archetypes.setting_hint: office building / hotel / parking garage after hours; fluorescent lights, identical doors, no people
key_beats: mundane-anchor: shortcut or routine errand
the space does not end where it should
narrator counts / marks / times to prove they are not imagining it
escape is ambiguous — they're out, but the space might be following
voice_patterns: spatial measurement language (steps, doors, minutes)
sensory fatigue (fluorescent hum, ventilation drone, footsteps)
never describe the 'thing' — only the space's wrongness

archetypes.setting_hint: basement / storage room / back-of-house corridor accessed by accident during a normal day
key_beats: mundane-anchor: utility task (supplies, maintenance, locked door)
narrator crosses a threshold that wasn't marked
space feels residential but is clearly not
narrator leaves before understanding what they saw
voice_patterns: utilitarian tone — narrator is not imaginative
describe textures (carpet, paint, dust) in disproportionate detail
end on an object the narrator took or left behind

archetypes.setting_hint: first-person account delivered to the Archivist as a voicemail, letter, or interview transcript — Archivist frames the retelling
key_beats: Archivist frame: 'this was shared with me by...' — vague witness identity
witness describes the encounter in their own idiom
Archivist notes a small detail the witness didn't emphasize
Archivist closes the frame without closure
voice_patterns: dual register: Archivist's measured tone + witness's colloquial voice
witness carries the emotion; Archivist stays detached
end with the Archivist referring to the witness in third person past tense

archetypes.setting_hint: narrator acquires or inherits an object (tape, photograph, letter, keychain); the object's history is unknown
key_beats: mundane-anchor: how the object came to the narrator
narrator examines the object, finds a small anomaly
anomaly suggests the object has a specific prior owner or context
narrator attempts to dispose of or return the object — the attempt fails or lingers
voice_patterns: physical-description detail on the object; sensory texture
no dialogue with the object; the object does not speak
end with the object present somewhere the narrator did not put it

archetypes.setting_hint: narrator was told 'rules of the place' upon arrival (security briefing, orientation, handover note); one rule is violated
key_beats: mundane-anchor: the job or task context
narrator recounts the rules — specifically and clearly
one rule is broken (by someone else, or by circumstance)
consequence manifests in a way that is strange but not violent
voice_patterns: procedural, almost HR-manual tone in early paragraphs
tone shifts slightly after the rule breaks
never explain WHY the rule existed

archetypes.setting_hint: commute, supermarket, gym, public transit — a stranger is unusually attentive to the narrator
key_beats: mundane-anchor: repetitive daily context
stranger displays knowledge of the narrator they should not have
narrator breaks the pattern (different route, store, time)
stranger appears on the new pattern too
voice_patterns: urban / suburban specificity
narrator's rationalization layer
end without confrontation — the stranger persists off-page
